title: Setting Up Power of Attorney: Steps to Take Now
summary: Setting up a POA is more straightforward than most families expect, and it's far easier to do proactively than under time pressure.
category: Life Transitions III: Legal & Financial Shifts
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Setting Up Power of Attorney: Steps to Take Now | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Setting up a POA is more straightforward than most families expect, and it's far easier to do proactively than under time pressure.
whatYouNeedToKnow: The person granting POA must have legal capacity at the time of signing — waiting too long can make this impossible.
An elder law attorney can draft a document tailored to your state and situation.
Copies should be shared with relevant institutions, not just kept in a drawer.

paragraphs: Capacity to sign a POA must exist at the time of signing, which is exactly why this conversation is easier to have early — once a cognitive decline has progressed significantly, it may be too late to execute a valid document, leaving families to pursue guardianship instead, a much longer and costlier process.

Once signed, banks, financial institutions, and medical providers often need their own copy on file to actually honor it — simply having the document at home isn't enough for it to function when needed.

nextSteps: Start this conversation well before it feels urgent.
Work with an elder law attorney familiar with your state's requirements.
Provide copies to banks, doctors, and other relevant institutions once signed.
disclaimer: This article is general information, not legal advice, and laws vary by state. The specific requirements, deadlines, and procedures described here may not apply exactly to your situation. Confirm all details with a licensed elder law attorney before acting, especially before signing, filing, or relying on any document.

sources.name: Getting Your Affairs in Order Checklist
